steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 1 says Asimov's characters are flat. The question asks how the author of Text 2 would respond to that claim. Text 2 argues that Asimov never intended to develop characters — his flat characterization serves a deliberate thematic purpose (conveying the insignificance of individuals across vast historical timescales).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "The flatness of Asimov's characters is a feature of *Robots and Empire* and *I, Robot* but not of *Foundation*.": Text 2 actually uses Foundation as its main example of flat characterization. This gets the facts backwards. ✗
  • "Asimov's characterizations would have been more believable if his writing had discussed fewer ideas.": Text 2 never suggests Asimov should have discussed fewer ideas — it defends his approach as intentional. ✗
  • "Asimov's characterizations would have been richer if he had possessed a deeper understanding of history.": Text 2 says flat characterization is central to his thematic aims around history — it doesn't suggest he lacked historical understanding. ✗
  • "The flatness of Asimov's characters should not necessarily be considered a flaw in his writing.": Text 2 explicitly argues Asimov's lack of characterization was deliberate and 'central to his thematic aims,' so calling it a flaw is wrong. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
